intro: 2003. The GameCube is a distant third behind Sony's PlayStation 2 and Microsoft's Xbox. The industry consensus is clear: win by having the most powerful console. You run Nintendo, and you're about to refuse to play that game.

realOutcome.summary: Nintendo stopped competing on horsepower and expanded gaming to everyone. Every 'what actually happened' below is real.

decisions.scenario: You're losing the console war on raw power. You can pour everything into out-speccing Sony next generation — or stop fighting for the same hardcore gamers and try to expand who plays games at all.

choices.text: Expand the audience. Stop fighting on horsepower.
choices.reveal: Iwata pushed a 'blue ocean' strategy: instead of chasing Sony's hardcore gamers, grow the market to people who'd never touched a controller. It reframed the entire competition.

choices.text: Out-spec Sony with a raw-power console.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You go toe-to-toe on teraflops and lose on cost, developer support, and exclusives. Third place in a war you chose to fight on the enemy's terms.

choices.text: Quit hardware and become a software publisher.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You give up the platform control that lets Mario and Zelda sell hardware. Nintendo becomes just another studio at the mercy of other people's boxes.

decisions.scenario: For your next handheld, Sony is coming with the PSP — a sleek, powerful media machine. You have a strange idea: two screens, one of them touch-controlled, marketed as a whole new 'third pillar.'

choices.reveal: The Nintendo DS, with its touchscreen and quirky games, became one of the best-selling game systems ever — over 150M units — and buried the more powerful PSP.

choices.text: Build a straightforward, more powerful Game Boy.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: A better Game Boy fights the PSP on the PSP's terms — specs and media. You win some ground but never open handhelds to a brand-new audience.

choices.text: Cede handhelds to Sony and focus on consoles.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You abandon the one market you dominate. Sony takes portables, and you've surrendered your safest, most profitable turf.

decisions.scenario: For the next console, your team proposes motion controls, a low-powered GPU, and a low price — codenamed 'Revolution.' It won't render cutting-edge graphics, and hardcore reviewers will mock it.

choices.text: Bet on motion controls and a cheap, weak box.
choices.reveal: The Wii launched in 2006 with motion controls and modest hardware. It outsold the far more powerful PS3 and Xbox 360 for years and put a console in living rooms that had never owned one.

choices.text: Build a graphics powerhouse to match the rivals.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You match Sony and Microsoft on power, sell at a loss like they do, and blur into a three-way spec war. The magic that made grandparents buy a console never happens.

choices.text: Ship a safe HD console with a normal controller.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: A competent, forgettable box. Without the motion gimmick there's no cultural moment, no Wii Sports phenomenon, no new audience.

decisions.scenario: Sony and Microsoft sell their consoles at a loss to grab market share, making the money back on games. You can price the Wii to actually profit on every unit at $250.

choices.text: Price it at $250 — profitable from unit one.
choices.reveal: Because the Wii used cheaper hardware, Nintendo made a profit on every console sold from day one — a rarity in the industry. It printed money while rivals bled on each unit.

choices.text: Sell at a loss to maximize install base.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You copy the rivals' loss-leader math and throw away the Wii's single biggest financial advantage. Growth you pay dearly for.

choices.text: Price it premium at $400 to signal quality.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: A $400 underpowered console is a terrible pitch. The mass-market buyers you were counting on stay home.

decisions.scenario: You could market the Wii to the usual hardcore gamers, or aim squarely at families, grandparents, and people who've never played — bundling a simple game called Wii Sports to show anyone can pick it up.

choices.text: Target families and non-gamers; bundle Wii Sports.
choices.reveal: Nintendo bundled Wii Sports and marketed to everyone from kids to grandparents. It became a social phenomenon, and Wii Sports one of the best-selling games of all time.

choices.text: Market only to core gamers like the rivals do.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: You fight for the same hardcore buyers with weaker hardware. The audience that made the Wii a cultural event never gets invited.

choices.text: Skip the pack-in game to protect software sales.
choices.reveal: ALTERNATE TIMELINE: Without Wii Sports in the box, first-time buyers have nothing that instantly shows what the console is for. The motion magic stays hidden in stores.

bands.line: You changed the game instead of losing it. Legend episode: WON.
A solid third-place console maker. Alive, but never the phenomenon.
You fought the spec war and lost it. 💀

endings.ctaLine: Nintendo 5x'd by refusing the arms race. Today's agents flagged a company winning a game nobody else is playing →
